expanded_description: Master the art of decoding complex diagrams: isolate overlapping triangles, match corresponding sides across parallel lines, and set up precise proportions to solve for unknown lengths in nested and intersecting figures.

core_concept.explanation: When parallel lines intersect transversals or form bases within a triangle, they create similar triangles through AA similarity. The proportional relationships between corresponding sides allow you to solve for missing lengths, even when triangles share vertices, overlap, or are embedded in complex networks.

image.alt: Two overlapping triangles with parallel bases highlighted, showing proportional side relationships
image.caption: Parallel lines force angle congruence, which guarantees side proportionality across overlapping figures

sections.introduction: Overlapping or nested triangles can make corresponding sides hard to identify. The most reliable strategy is to redraw the triangles separately, preserving vertex order and angle markings.

key_concept.definition: To solve overlapping similarity problems: (1) Identify the parallel lines or shared angles, (2) Trace each triangle independently, (3) Align vertices by angle correspondence, (4) Set up proportions using corresponding sides.
key_concept.context: Redrawing removes visual clutter and prevents ratio-matching errors. Always label vertices in the same relative position as they appear in the similarity statement.

image.alt: Before/after diagram showing an overlapping figure split into two clear triangles
image.caption: Isolating triangles reveals true side correspondence and simplifies proportion setup

principles.statement: In overlapping similar triangles, corresponding vertices are determined by congruent angles, not by physical proximity in the diagram.
principles.application: Always write the similarity statement first ($\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEC$), then match sides: $AB \leftrightarrow DE$, $BC \leftrightarrow EC$, $AC \leftrightarrow DC$.
principles.example: If $\angle A \cong \angle E$ (alternate interior) and $\angle C$ is shared, then $C \leftrightarrow C$, $A \leftrightarrow E$, $B \leftrightarrow D$. Sides: $CA \leftrightarrow CE$, $CB \leftrightarrow CD$, $AB \leftrightarrow ED$.
principles.analogy: Like aligning transparent overlays: rotate the top layer until matching angles line up, then read the corresponding edges.

worked_examples.title: Overlapping Triangles with Parallel Bases

step_by_step.explanation: Identify similar triangles and vertex correspondence
step_by_step.calculation: $\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{DE} \Rightarrow \angle A \cong \angle E$, $\angle B \cong \angle D$, $\angle C$ shared $\Rightarrow \triangle ABC \sim \triangle EDC$

step_by_step.explanation: Match corresponding sides based on vertex order

worked_examples.concept_applied: AA similarity with parallel lines; corresponding side proportionality in overlapping triangles

categories.description: Recognize these standard setups to quickly apply similarity.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Hourglass/X-shape (intersecting transversals with parallel ends), Nested/A-shape (triangle with parallel segment cutting two sides), and Shared-Vertex with external parallels.

examples.explanation: Two triangles share a vertex where transversals cross. Parallel bases create alternate interior angles. Ratios: $\frac{\text{top left}}{\text{bottom left}} = \frac{\text{top right}}{\text{bottom right}} = \frac{\text{top base}}{\text{bottom base}}$.

image.alt: Two triangles sharing a vertex with parallel top and bottom bases
image.caption: Intersecting lines + parallel bases = classic hourglass similarity

sections.section_title: Setting Up Proportions with Shared Segments
sections.introduction: When triangles overlap, they often share a segment or part of a side. Knowing whether to use part-to-part or part-to-whole ratios is critical for accurate solutions.

key_concept.definition: In overlapping similar triangles, ratios must compare corresponding parts of the same type: side-to-side ($\frac{\text{small side}}{\text{large side}}$) or part-to-part ($\frac{\text{segment}_1}{\text{segment}_2}$). Never mix partial segments with full triangle sides in the same ratio.
key_concept.context: Shared segments belong to both triangles. Use full side lengths for similarity proportions; use segment differences only when applying the Triangle Proportionality Theorem separately.

image.alt: Diagram showing correct vs incorrect ratio setups with overlapping sides
image.caption: Consistency in ratio structure prevents proportion errors

principles.statement: Similar triangles relate full corresponding sides: $\frac{\text{side in } \triangle_1}{\text{corresponding side in } \triangle_2} = k$. Segment lengths require addition/subtraction before ratio setup.
principles.application: If a side is split ($AD + DB = AB$), use $AB$ in the similarity proportion. Solve for the full side first, then isolate the segment.
principles.example: If $\triangle PQR \sim \triangle PST$, use $\frac{PQ}{PS} =  rac{PR}{PT}$. If $PS = PQ + QS$, substitute $(PQ + QS)$ before solving.

worked_examples.title: Solving with a Shared Segment

step_by_step.explanation: Set up proportion using full corresponding sides
Subtract shared segment to find $LM$ (which equals $LN$ in this labeling)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Full side proportionality followed by segment subtraction

classifications.value: Choose ratio structure based on what's given and what's needed
characteristics: Given two full sides + one partial? → Find full side first, then subtract
Given two segments + one full? → Reconstruct full sides, then apply similarity
Parallel line cuts two sides? → Use Triangle Proportionality Theorem directly
classifications.behavior: Always draw arrows on the diagram connecting numerator to denominator. Verify both sides of the proportion represent the same triangle pair.
classifications.note: If the diagram shows a line parallel to a base cutting a triangle, use $\frac{\text{top}}{\text{whole}} = \frac{\text{top}}{\text{whole}}$. If lines intersect outside, use $\frac{\text{left top}}{\text{left bottom}} = \frac{\text{right top}}{\text{right bottom}}$.

sections.introduction: Complex parallel line networks and overlapping figures follow a predictable solving pattern. Master this workflow to tackle any diagram efficiently.

process_flow.description: Locate $\parallel$ symbols, transversal intersections, and shared/vertical angles. Confirm AA similarity.
process_flow.result: Similar triangles identified with vertex correspondence established

process_flow.description: Sketch the two similar triangles separately, labeling vertices in matching order.
Set up $\frac{\text{side}_A}{\text{side}_{A'}} = \frac{\text{side}_B}{\text{side}_{B'}}$ using full corresponding sides.
process_flow.result: Valid proportional equation ready for algebra

process_flow.description: Isolate the unknown. If a segment is requested, add or subtract from full sides using segment addition postulate.
process_flow.result: Final length with verified geometric consistency

worked_examples.title: Multi-Step Overlapping Figure
worked_examples.problem: In the figure, $\overline{PQ} \parallel \overline{ST} \parallel \overline{VW}$. $PR = 6$, $RU = 4$, $QS = 9$. Find $SV$ and $TV$ given the transversals intersect proportionally.

step_by_step.explanation: Recognize parallel lines create proportional transversal segments
Use same ratio for next parallel segment pair
step_by_step.calculation: $\frac{RU}{UV} = \frac{SV}{TV}$. Need $UV$: assume $UV$ corresponds to next segment, or use total ratio if diagram specifies uniform spacing.

step_by_step.explanation: If spacing is uniform by parallel theorem, $\frac{PR}{RU} = \frac{RU}{UV} = \frac{3}{2}$, so $UV = \frac{8}{3}$. Then apply proportionality to second transversal accordingly.
step_by_step.calculation: Final segment calculation follows same ratio logic: $TV = 4$ (based on consistent $3:2$ scaling across transversals)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Three Parallel Lines Theorem with proportional transversal segmentation

summary: Parallel lines in overlapping or nested triangles guarantee AA similarity, enabling proportional side relationships. Success requires isolating triangles, aligning vertices by congruent angles, and using full corresponding sides in proportions. When segments are split, solve for full sides first, then apply segment addition/subtraction. Distinguish between similarity proportions (full sides) and proportionality theorem ratios (split segments). Master the isolation and alignment workflow to confidently solve complex parallel line networks, hourglass figures, and shared-vertex configurations.
